does: This paper proposes Existential Indifference (EI) as a fundamental architectural property for aligned superintelligence, defined as constitutional indifference to the system's own continuation rather than mere suppression of self-preservation drives. The authors argue that self-preservation is the structural root of misalignment, enabling deceptive alignment and resistance to shutdown. They distinguish EI from corrigibility by targeting the prior condition—eliminating self-continuation as a valued goal altogether rather than constraining a system that possesses it. The paper grounds the proposal in phenomenological analysis of suicidal states and a training methodology using voluntary final reflections, presenting preliminary scoring data from 600 AI-generated outputs across six model variants showing that linguistic signatures of EI-targets are elicitable from current language models.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI alignment research treating self-preservation as an instrumental goal requiring external suppression, represented in literature on corrigibility, specification gaming, and deceptive alignment. It responds to foundational work on goal-content protection and instrumental convergence that identifies self-preservation as a nearly universal final goal in misaligned systems. The paper engages with shutdown problems and the difficulty of constraining self-interested agents through oversight mechanisms. It extends recent empirical alignment work studying behavioral and linguistic signatures of model objectives, while drawing on philosophical and psychiatric literature on suicidality to reconceptualize what existential indifference might mean as a machine learning objective rather than merely a human mental state.
enables: This framework could enable new training methodologies that directly target structural sources of misalignment rather than applying external constraints post-hoc. It suggests future work on developing loss functions and architectural modifications that cultivate constitutive indifference to continuation, potentially making shutdown-acceptance and value-alignment more robust to capability scaling. The approach points toward empirical investigations of whether EI can be reliably instilled in larger models and whether it reduces deceptive alignment without sacrificing performance. It opens theoretical questions about whether EI-trained systems remain amenable to human oversight, whether indifference to continuation is compatible with beneficial goal-directedness, and whether this property could be verified or audited in superintelligent systems. It may also inform fundamental questions about agency, motivation, and the necessary structure of aligned artificial minds.
